locations.name: Al Quaa Desert
locations.desc: One of the darkest skies in the Emirates. Deep in the southern desert, far from any city glow, it's a magnet for stargazers and astrophotographers chasing the Milky Way.
locations.body:
Al Quaa is the darkest accessible desert in the UAE and arguably the best place to view the Milky Way galaxy in the country. Set deep in the southern sands of Abu Dhabi, far from any city light, it's a pilgrimage spot for stargazers and astrophotographers — especially on clear, moonless nights between October and March.

The bright core of the Milky Way rises over an empty horizon here, with almost zero light pollution to wash it out. Come prepared for a remote, self-sufficient overnight: a capable 4x4, plenty of water, warm layers for cold desert nights and a red-light torch to protect your night vision. There are no facilities — which is exactly why the skies are so spectacular.

locations.name: Al Rafisah Dam
locations.desc: An emerald reservoir ringed by the Hajar mountains near Khorfakkan, with ridge hikes, kayaking and a family-friendly lakeside park on Sharjah's east coast.
locations.body:
Al Rafisah Dam sits in the rugged Hajar mountains just outside Khorfakkan on Sharjah's east coast, where a calm, emerald reservoir is hemmed in by dramatic peaks. It has become one of the east coast's most popular outdoor escapes — a place to hike the surrounding ridges, paddle a kayak or pedal boat on the water, and picnic by the lake with mountains rising on every side.

The hiking is the draw for adventurers. Trails climb from the dam into the mountains for sweeping views over the water and out toward the Gulf of Oman. The landscaped park at the base is easy and family-friendly, while the ridge routes are more demanding, so come prepared with plenty of water, sun protection and good footwear.

The cooler months from October to April are the time to visit, when the mountain air is pleasant and the light on the water is at its best. The dam is roughly two hours from Dubai via the scenic east-coast roads, making it a rewarding day trip or a stop on a Khorfakkan weekend.

locations.name: Big Red
locations.desc: The most iconic dune in the UAE. Towering red sand dunes perfect for sandboarding and 4x4 adventures. Best visited at sunrise for dramatic golden light.

locations.name: Crescent Moon Lake
locations.desc: A quiet desert lake where Arabian oryx, gazelle and deer come to drink at dawn and dusk. One of the best spots near the city for wildlife and birdlife.
locations.body:
Crescent Moon Lake is a quiet desert lake on the edge of Dubai, ringed by reeds and soft dunes and known for its wildlife. At dawn and dusk you can spot Arabian oryx, gazelle and deer coming to drink, along with rich birdlife — making it one of the best easy-access spots near the city for nature watching.

It's a calm, scenic place for a low-key camp or picnic, with that rare feeling of wilderness just a short drive from town. Keep your distance from the animals, take all litter home, and arrive early for the best sightings and softest light.

locations.name: Desert Camping (Lake View)
locations.desc: A hidden, little-known camping spot tucked beside a desert lake — calm water, soft dunes and almost no crowds. A local secret for a peaceful overnight.
locations.body:
This is one of the UAE's best-kept secrets — a secluded desert camping spot tucked beside a quiet lake, with calm water, soft dunes and almost no crowds. For anyone hunting secluded camping spots in the UAE, it offers the kind of stillness and privacy the busier Al Qudra lakes have lost.

There are no facilities, so come fully self-sufficient with water, shade and a pack-it-out mindset. Sunset over the water and a quiet night under the stars are the whole point. A high-clearance vehicle helps you reach the more private corners away from the tracks.

locations.name: Dibba Rock
locations.desc: World-class snorkelling and diving on the East Coast. Crystal clear water, vibrant coral, and abundant marine life.

locations.name: Half Desert
locations.desc: Gentle, rolling dunes close to the city — the perfect place to air down and learn desert driving before tackling the bigger stuff.
locations.body:
Half Desert is a gentle stretch of rolling dunes close to Dubai, making it one of the best places in the UAE to learn desert driving. The soft, forgiving sand and modest dune heights are far less intimidating than the giants at Liwa, so it's a popular spot for first-timers to practise airing down, reading the terrain and building confidence.

Head out with a tyre deflator, a basic recovery kit and ideally a more experienced convoy. Late afternoon brings cooler sand and golden light across the dunes. It's a quick, easy escape from the city for an evening of off-roading.

locations.name: Hatta Mountains
locations.desc: Dubai's hidden mountain retreat. Kayak the turquoise dam, mountain bike through trails, or camp under stars. A complete outdoor escape.

locations.name: Jabal Yanas
locations.desc: A rugged peak above the east coast known for surreal, fiery sunsets and panoramic mountain views — a dramatic spot to camp high above the valleys.
locations.body:
Jabal Yanas is a rugged peak rising above the UAE's east coast near Khorfakkan, prized for its surreal, fiery sunsets and panoramic Hajar mountain views. The drive and short scramble to the high points reward you with one of the most dramatic vantage points in the country.

It's a popular spot for sunset camping — pitch up on a flat shoulder, watch the light turn the ridges copper and violet, then enjoy clear mountain skies after dark. Bring proper footwear, plenty of water and a head for heights; the views over the coast and surrounding peaks are unforgettable.

locations.name: Jebel Hafeet
locations.desc: The second highest peak in the UAE. A spectacular winding road climbs 1,240m with panoramic views over Al Ain and the desert beyond.

locations.name: Liwa Oasis
locations.desc: Home to some of the largest sand dunes on earth. The Empty Quarter starts here. An otherworldly landscape of mega-dunes and silence.

locations.name: Love Lake
locations.desc: Two heart-shaped lakes carved into the Al Qudra desert, fringed with greenery and birdlife. A short, easy drive from the city straight into the wilderness.
locations.body:
Love Lake is a pair of giant heart-shaped lakes carved into the Al Qudra desert outside Dubai, fringed with greenery, palm trees and abundant birdlife. It's one of the easiest ways to swap the city for genuine wilderness — a short, paved drive delivers you to flamingos, swans and quiet picnic spots.

The lakes are a popular sunrise and sunset destination for walking, cycling and casual camping nearby. Facilities are minimal, so bring your own water and supplies, and take everything home to keep this green oasis pristine.

locations.name: Mleiha Desert
locations.desc: An archaeological desert of golden dunes and wind-carved rock outcrops. Otherworldly formations, ancient tombs and Fossil Rock make it feel like another planet.
locations.body:
The Mleiha desert in Sharjah is one of the UAE's most otherworldly landscapes — golden dunes broken by wind-carved rock outcrops, ancient tombs and the famous Fossil Rock. It's a designated archaeological area where the scenery feels almost lunar, drawing photographers, dune drivers and history lovers alike.

You can self-drive the dunes (technical in places), explore the visitor centre and Bronze Age sites, or simply chase the light around the rock formations at sunset. An easy hour from Dubai, it pairs a striking desert backdrop with a real sense of the region's deep history.

locations.name: Shuweihat Island
locations.desc: A remote western island famous for surreal mushroom-shaped rock formations sculpted by wind and sea — raw, sculptural and gloriously empty.
locations.body:
Shuweihat Island, off the UAE's far western coast in Abu Dhabi's Al Dhafra region, is famous for surreal mushroom-shaped rock formations sculpted over millennia by wind and sea. Remote and rarely visited, it's a dream for photographers and adventurers chasing raw, sculptural landscapes.

Reaching it is a proper expedition — long distances, soft sand and full self-sufficiency required — but the reward is a coastline that feels untouched. Plan carefully, travel in convoy, and time your visit for the cooler months when the desert and coast are at their most forgiving.

locations.name: Sir Bani Yas Island
locations.desc: A natural island and pioneering wildlife reserve, home to free-roaming Arabian oryx, giraffe and cheetah as part of a decades-long conservation effort.
locations.body:
Sir Bani Yas Island is a natural island and pioneering wildlife reserve off Abu Dhabi's western coast, home to thousands of free-roaming animals including Arabian oryx, giraffes, cheetahs and gazelles. Part of a decades-long conservation effort, the Arabian Wildlife Park offers a genuine safari within the UAE.

Access is by boat or short flight, usually as a day trip or as a stay at the island's resorts. Beyond the wildlife drives you'll find mangroves, salt domes, archaeological sites and quiet beaches — a unique blend of conservation, history and coastline found nowhere else in the country.

locations.name: Snoopy Island
locations.desc: A rocky island off Al Aqah named for its dog-like profile, ringed by coral and home to turtles, reef sharks and clouds of tropical fish — the UAE's most beloved shore snorkel.
locations.body:
Snoopy Island is the UAE's most popular shore snorkeling spot, lying just off Al Aqah beach on the Fujairah coast. Named for its resemblance to the cartoon dog lying on its back, the island is wrapped in shallow coral reef you can reach by swimming straight out from the sand — no boat required.

Expect green turtles, blacktip reef sharks, parrotfish and clouds of sergeant majors in clear, calm water that's ideal for beginners. Go early on a weekday for the best visibility and fewest crowds, bring your own mask and fins, and wear reef-safe sunscreen. It's an easy day trip from Dubai or Sharjah and ranks among the best snorkeling experiences in the UAE.

locations.name: Wadi Shab
locations.desc: One of the most beautiful wadis in the region. Hike through a narrow gorge, swim through turquoise pools and discover a hidden waterfall cave.

locations.name: Wadi Showka
locations.desc: A mountain wadi in the Hajar foothills with rock pools, shaded trails and sweeping ridge views — a favourite for hiking, biking and easy camping.
locations.body:
Wadi Showka sits in the Hajar foothills of Ras Al Khaimah, an accessible mountain wadi laced with rock pools, shaded gravel trails and dam-fed streams. It's a favourite for easy hiking, mountain biking and beginner-friendly camping, with sweeping ridge views and a network of marked paths.

After rain the pools fill and the wadi comes alive; in the cooler months it's a perfect half-day escape or relaxed overnight under the mountains. A 2WD reaches the main areas, though a high-clearance vehicle opens up more of the trails.

packing.name: Phone charged + share live location
packing.note: Tell someone your route & return time
